It's just a basket of stocks that you can buy as a single stock.

As long as we're on definitions here's an email question from Arlene.
If a single stock is rated as overweight, what does that mean?
MR.
GARDNER: Well, often it's the analyst, actually, who rates the stock in my experience.

But I think what that analyst means to say is that within your portfolio you should, assuming you have, let's say, 20 different investments, you should tend to overweight the amount of money that you would put in that particular investment.
Whether or not you agree with the analyst or whether the analyst is good or not is a separate question.
But I think that's what's intended by that particular Wall Street term.

At our site caps, you can click on the tag environmentally friendly and find about 14, 15 companies that are using community intelligence believes having researched then that they are environmentally friendly.
And then you can sort them and look which are the higher rated ones.
But I think, overall it's worth noting that not all of these technologies are the same.
And so while the ETF will buy all of it, in many cases, we start looking at the balance sheet of the company to see if they have more cash than debt.
Do they actually have any sales at all?
There are a lot of fly-by-night companies that are going to rush when they ethanol is a big thing and the government is subsidizing and all of a sudden, you know, they're taking their company public.

We're talking with Motley Fool David Gardner with us from Fool Global Headquarters in Alexandria Virginia.
You're listening to TALK OF THE NATION from NPR News.
And the big new story this week, obviously, the newspaper business has been in some decline over the past several years.
Rupert Murdoch made a $5 billion bid for Dow Jones and Co., which publishes the Wall Street Journal, among other things.
Is this the time to be looking at newspapers?
Is this a good investment?
Well, I think when you understand what Fox is hoping to do which is to launch a successful business news cable channel in competition with CNBC.

You might see, if you have the strategic mind that I think Rupert Murdoch has actually demonstrated that he does have over the last 20 years, you might see that it's a good thing potentially, to have the Wall Street Journal be a significant brand for you and your network.
And so I think that that is strategic thinking it certainly has not been a great business, Neal, as you committed to earlier.
The newspaper business, a lot of it, has been in decline or just sort of steady state but no real growth.
But the Wall Street Journal, at least in Rupert Murdoch's mind is worth 60 percent more than stock market was according it in its value just last week.
So usually, when investors and shareholders see an attractive premium prize like that, they jump at it.
In this case, as you well know, it was not necessarily welcome.
It was an external offer and shareholders will have to consider it.
I know one thing.
If I were a shareholder, I'd be taking that deal.
Moving from old media to new, Amazon.com jumps from about $45 to over $60 in just a matter of a few days.
Is, if you held stock at Amazon, would this be a tine to consider the old adage, sell high?
Well.
Here I've issued a disclaimer because I am a shareholder of Amazon.
It doesn't able me briefly to brag, Neal, because I've actually just bought and held ever since back in the day.
So, you know, a stock I bought at three, it went to 95, I didn't sell, it went down to seven, I didn't sell.
And today, as you mentioned, it's about 60.

You end up with days where if you hold and show patience as an investor - if you know the company, you trust the management and you like the growth story, you hold for a decade - you'll have days where the stock will rise more in a single day than you paid for your cost basis.
And I'm happy, at least in my little disclaimer here - and we Fools make lots of mistakes, too so I don't want to sound like a bragger.
But I will say that when the stock went up $10 the other day that was three times what I paid for 10 years ago.
What I sell?
No. I'm not going to sell my Amazon and I would suggest - you don't have to go out and buy it.
But any company that can grow its sales 32 percent - this March quarter just closed - you know, from $2.2 to almost $3 billion.
I mean, that's stacking on a tremendous amount of growth in its 10th, 12th year of business.
I think, you know, they're starting those sell-Starbucks coffee.
They're adding groceries, kind of what Wal-Mart did, You know, recently to its business about 10 years ago.
Now, as you know, Wal-Mart is the number one grosser in America.
But I just think Amazon is so smart and they have a lot of potential.

But if you're not actually - if it's not a great big fun board game for you, which it is for me, then I think that the index fund is a wonderful way to start a $15,000 investment for over the course of your long lives together.
